source: a16z Podcast — Is there an AI bubble?
summary: AI frontier model gross margins will be structurally lower than SaaS/internet era margins indefinitely
summary_extended: Baker argues that frontier AI model gross margins will be structurally and permanently lower than the 80-90% gross margins characteristic of traditional SaaS and internet-era software businesses, due to the ongoing relevance of scaling laws and test-time compute requirements. The core reasoning is that inference and training compute costs are not a temporary inefficiency but a fundamental feature of how frontier models work — as long as scaling laws hold and test-time compute remains important, the cost structure cannot converge to SaaS-like economics. Baker explicitly states he does not see scaling laws or test-time compute dynamics changing, making this a structural rather than cyclical view. The investment implication is that frontier AI labs — including those embedded within large-cap tech companies — should not be valued on SaaS-equivalent margin assumptions, and any valuation model that extrapolates SaaS-like gross margins to AI model businesses is fundamentally flawed. Baker acknowledges lower opex may partially offset the gross margin drag, but the structural gap versus legacy SaaS remains.
detail: Due to scaling laws and the importance of test-time compute, frontier AI labs will have structurally lower gross margins than traditional SaaS (80-90%) for the foreseeable future. Baker does not see scaling laws or test-time compute dynamics changing. Lower opex may partially offset this but gross margins will remain fundamentally different.
confidence_evidence: Baker states this as structural fact tied to scaling laws, not a cyclical view
quote_fragment: gross margins are fundamentally different and until scaling laws change

ticker_audit.reasoning: Google DeepMind operates a frontier AI lab (Gemini) and Google Cloud sells frontier model inference; this thesis directly affects how GOOGL's AI segment should be valued on a margin basis.
Microsoft is deeply embedded in frontier AI via OpenAI partnership and Azure AI services; the structural margin thesis directly applies to its AI model economics and valuation.
Meta operates a frontier AI lab (Llama series) and is investing heavily in scaling compute; the structural gross margin thesis applies directly to its AI model business.
Amazon operates frontier AI via Anthropic investment and AWS Bedrock; the structural margin thesis applies to its AI model economics and cloud AI segment valuation.
